reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper tackles the task of open-world instance segmentation in images (no predefined set of classes). The proposed method extends on an existing state-of-the-art single-stage (without employing a dedicated proposals procedure) approach, such as Mask2former, through the addition of a foreground segmentation branch. The foreground prediction map is used as an auxiliary supervisory signal for the instance prediction branch to increase the consistency among these maps. The consistency loss applied on top of these predictions also alleviates the problem of incomplete instance annotation which could be considered a core contribution of this work (working with labeled and unlabeled data simultaneously - semi-supervised scenario).
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
* The tackled problem is challenging and of interest to the research community - open-world instance segmentation is far from being solved - since it implies the discovery of novel object instances that were not seen during training

Weaknesses:
* Problem statement description could be improved. The introductory section does not properly address the tackled task or the way in which this work contributes to current issues. 
* Hard to grasp from the paper what are the authors' contributions - I did not find the paper an easy read.
* The authors rename the Mask2former architecture as SOIS and wrongfully consider it their contribution since the architecture itself did not change as the authors stated themselves but rather the scenario in which it was used (extension to a semi-supervised scenario by employing a proxy constraint, in the form of a consistency loss between predicted masks of two existing branches in the architecture)

Other comments:
* Benchmarks are not referenced when first mentioned - same goes for COCO and UVO in the abstract - at least mention that these are datasets. 
* Figure 1 is crowded and hard to follow all of its parts. It would make more sense to split it into two parts - a, b, c - Part 1 and d, e, f, g - Part 2. In (b) consistency is misspelled and Cityscapes is misspelled in the figure caption.
* Figure 1c, 1d, 1e and 1f are not referenced in the paper.
* Figure 2 is not informative at all - What is the purpose of that backbone? - What is the format of the output? What is the input of the FCN in the foreground prediction branch and why not use the RGB image? The image caption does not complement the figure - most of the notations are not explained and the flow of the different branches is very hard to follow. Also, sentences such as "the foreground prediction branch segments a foreground region" can be derived from the naming of these components. Authors should highlight only the information that cannot be inferred solely from the naming.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity - Could be improved. The figure and caption do not complement each other. Also, there are notations in the paper that are not clearly defined (Section 3.2) What is Ki? The number of annotated object instances in a given image i? Should be explicitly specified.

Quality - The paper could be improved in terms of language and structure - there are also a number of typos and missing references that should be addressed. Most parts of Figure 1 are not referenced in the paper.

Novelty - I do not consider the contribution novel, but an extension of an existing state-of-the-art that was applied in a different learning setup that deals with missing annotations - not the fully-supervised case. For this purpose alone the authors apply a consistency loss computed as a segmentation loss between the instance segmentation branch and a foreground prediction branch and use this as a constraint in learning when labels are missing. 

Reproducibility - The authors have released the code in their supplementary material - the experiments should be easy to reproduce.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Besides the aforementioned issues with the current submission, I do not find it fit for publication not just for the structure or lack of clarity, but mostly due to its incremental contribution to existing works.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper tackles the task of open-world instance segmentation in images (no predefined set of classes). The proposed method extends on an existing state-of-the-art single-stage (without employing a dedicated proposals procedure) approach, such as Mask2former, through the addition of a foreground segmentation branch. The foreground prediction map is used as an auxiliary supervisory signal for the instance prediction branch to increase the consistency among these maps. The consistency loss applied on top of these predictions also alleviates the problem of incomplete instance annotation which could be considered a core contribution of this work (working with labeled and unlabeled data simultaneously - semi-supervised scenario).

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
* The tackled problem is challenging and of interest to the research community - open-world instance segmentation is far from being solved - since it implies the discovery of novel object instances that were not seen during training

Weaknesses:
* Problem statement description could be improved. The introductory section does not properly address the tackled task or the way in which this work contributes to current issues. 
* Hard to grasp from the paper what are the authors' contributions - I did not find the paper an easy read.
* The authors rename the Mask2former architecture as SOIS and wrongfully consider it their contribution since the architecture itself did not change as the authors stated themselves but rather the scenario in which it was used (extension to a semi-supervised scenario by employing a proxy constraint, in the form of a consistency loss between predicted masks of two existing branches in the architecture)

Other comments:
* Benchmarks are not referenced when first mentioned - same goes for COCO and UVO in the abstract - at least mention that these are datasets. 
* Figure 1 is crowded and hard to follow all of its parts. It would make more sense to split it into two parts - a, b, c - Part 1 and d, e, f, g - Part 2. In (b) consistency is misspelled and Cityscapes is misspelled in the figure caption.
* Figure 1c, 1d, 1e and 1f are not referenced in the paper.
* Figure 2 is not informative at all - What is the purpose of that backbone? - What is the format of the output? What is the input of the FCN in the foreground prediction branch and why not use the RGB image? The image caption does not complement the figure - most of the notations are not explained and the flow of the different branches is very hard to follow. Also, sentences such as "the foreground prediction branch segments a foreground region" can be derived from the naming of these components. Authors should highlight only the information that cannot be inferred solely from the naming.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity - Could be improved. The figure and caption do not complement each other. Also, there are notations in the paper that are not clearly defined (Section 3.2) What is Ki? The number of annotated object instances in a given image i? Should be explicitly specified.

Quality - The paper could be improved in terms of language and structure - there are also a number of typos and missing references that should be addressed. Most parts of Figure 1 are not referenced in the paper.

Novelty - I do not consider the contribution novel, but an extension of an existing state-of-the-art that was applied in a different learning setup that deals with missing annotations - not the fully-supervised case. For this purpose alone the authors apply a consistency loss computed as a segmentation loss between the instance segmentation branch and a foreground prediction branch and use this as a constraint in learning when labels are missing. 

Reproducibility - The authors have released the code in their supplementary material - the experiments should be easy to reproduce.

Summary Of The Review:
Besides the aforementioned issues with the current submission, I do not find it fit for publication not just for the structure or lack of clarity, but mostly due to its incremental contribution to existing works.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Paper Summary:**  
This work addresses open-world instance segmentation, where objects are not constrained to predefined classes. The approach builds upon Mask2former—a state-of-the-art single-stage method—by integrating a foreground segmentation branch. This branch generates an auxiliary prediction map, which is leveraged through a consistency loss applied to the instance prediction branch. This loss aims to enhance prediction coherence and crucially mitigates issues arising from incomplete instance annotations. The method effectively operates in a semi-supervised setting, handling both labeled and unlabeled data simultaneously.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
**Strengths:**  
*   The core problem—open-world instance segmentation—is highly challenging and relevant to the community. Discovering unseen object instances during inference remains an unsolved research frontier, making this a worthwhile pursuit.

**Weaknesses:**  
*   The problem statement and introductory section lack clarity. They insufficiently define the specific task addressed and fail to articulate precisely how this work tackles current limitations in the field.
*   The paper's contributions are difficult to discern and the manuscript is not easily digestible.
*   The authors inappropriately claim Mask2former as their own contribution by renaming it SOIS. As they themselves acknowledge, the core architecture is unchanged. The actual contribution lies in adapting it to a semi-supervised scenario via a proxy constraint: a consistency loss applied between the predicted masks of the existing instance branch and the newly added foreground branch.

**Detailed Comments:**  
*   Benchmarks (e.g., COCO, UVO mentioned in the abstract) should be clearly identified as datasets upon first reference.
*   Figure 1 is overly complex. Consider splitting it: Part 1 (a, b, c) and Part 2 (d, e, f, g). Note misspellings ("consistency" in (b), "Cityscapes" in the caption). Components (c), (d), (e), and (f) lack references in the main text.
*   Figure 2 is uninformative. The caption and figure fail to explain: the backbone's purpose, the output format, the input source for the FCN in the foreground branch (and why the RGB image isn't used), or the flow between branches. The caption merely restates obvious information derivable from branch names (e.g., "the foreground prediction branch segments a foreground region"). It should focus on conveying non-intuitive details and explaining the notations used.
*   Clarity requires improvement: Figure/caption mismatches exist, and notations lack definitions (e.g., `K_i` in Section 3.2 – is it the count of annotated instances in image `i`? This must be explicitly stated).

**Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**  
*   **Clarity:** Needs enhancement, particularly regarding figure integration and definition of terms/notations.
*   **Quality:** The language and structure could be refined. Typos exist and references are missing (especially for Figure 1 parts). These issues require attention.
*   **Novelty:** The contribution is incremental. It adapts an existing state-of-the-art architecture (Mask2former) to a semi-supervised learning setup addressing missing annotations. The core mechanism is the application of a consistency loss (segmentation loss) between the existing instance segmentation branch and a newly added foreground prediction branch, serving as a constraint for learning with incomplete labels.
*   **Reproducibility:** Good. Code is provided in the supplementary material, facilitating experiment replication.

**Overall Assessment:**  
While the technical approach to handling incomplete annotations via consistency loss in a semi-supervised setting is noted, significant concerns remain. These include issues with clarity, structure, presentation (especially figures), and, most critically, the framing of contributions. The work represents an incremental adaptation of Mask2former rather than a fundamentally novel contribution.

**Rating:**  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper tackles the task of open-world instance segmentation in images (no predefined set of classes). The proposed method extends on an existing state-of-the-art single-stage (without employing a dedicated proposals procedure) approach, such as Mask2former, through the addition of a foreground segmentation branch. The foreground prediction map is used as an auxiliary supervisory signal for the instance prediction branch to increase the consistency among these maps. The consistency loss applied on top of these predictions also alleviates the problem of incomplete instance annotation which could be considered a core contribution of this work (working with labeled and unlabeled data simultaneously - semi-supervised scenario).

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
* The tackled problem is challenging and of interest to the research community - open-world instance segmentation is far from being solved - since it implies the discovery of novel object instances that were not seen during training

Weaknesses:
* Problem statement description could be improved. The introductory section does not properly address the tackled task or the way in which this work contributes to current issues. 
* Hard to grasp from the paper what are the authors' contributions - I did not find the paper an easy read.
* The authors rename the Mask2former architecture as SOIS and wrongfully consider it their contribution since the architecture itself did not change as the authors stated themselves but rather the scenario in which it was used (extension to a semi-supervised scenario by employing a proxy constraint, in the form of a consistency loss between predicted masks of two existing branches in the architecture)

Other comments:
* Benchmarks are not referenced when first mentioned - same goes for COCO and UVO in the abstract - at least mention that these are datasets. 
* Figure 1 is crowded and hard to follow all of its parts. It would make more sense to split it into two parts - a, b, c - Part 1 and d, e, f, g - Part 2. In (b) consistency is misspelled and Cityscapes is misspelled in the figure caption.
* Figure 1c, 1d, 1e and 1f are not referenced in the paper.
* Figure 2 is not informative at all - What is the purpose of that backbone? - What is the format of the output? What is the input of the FCN in the foreground prediction branch and why not use the RGB image? The image caption does not complement the figure - most of the notations are not explained and the flow of the different branches is very hard to follow. Also, sentences such as "the foreground prediction branch segments a foreground region" can be derived from the naming of these components. Authors should highlight only the information that cannot be inferred solely from the naming.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity - Could be improved. The figure and caption do not complement each other. Also, there are notations in the paper that are not clearly defined (Section 3.2) What is Ki? The number of annotated object instances in a given image i? Should be explicitly specified.

Quality - The paper could be improved in terms of language and structure - there are also a number of typos and missing references that should be addressed. Most parts of Figure 1 are not referenced in the paper.

Novelty - I do not consider the contribution novel, but an extension of an existing state-of-the-art that was applied in a different learning setup that deals with missing annotations - not the fully-supervised case. For this purpose alone the authors apply a consistency loss computed as a segmentation loss between the instance segmentation branch and a foreground prediction branch and use this as a constraint in learning when labels are missing. 

Reproducibility - The authors have released the code in their supplementary material - the experiments should be easy to reproduce.

Summary Of The Review:
Besides the aforementioned issues with the current submission, I do not find it fit for publication not just for the structure or lack of clarity, but mostly due to its incremental contribution to existing works.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes an open world instance segmentation framework, which contains three stages: 1) it proposes an auxiliary task of predicting total foreground on the basis of existing query-based mask2former model. 2) it includes a cross-task consistency loss and improve results by correcting outputs between two tasks. 3) the paper also designs a way of adding pseudo labels on COCO, which gain some improvement on open-world settings, including semi-supervised setting.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Pros:

1. The paper proposes a single-stage open-world Instance segmentation framework based on Mask2Former.
2. Adding an auxiliary task and a consistency loss for self-supervised learning, which improves a lot on both UVO-to-UVO and COCO-to-UVO settings.
3. Experiments demonstrate the semi-supervised framework can achieve remarkable performance with much smaller amount of labeled data.

Cons:
1. Although the paper proposed a new constraint called “cross-task consistency loss” for OWIS problem, it lacks of sufficient validation by either theoretical interpretation or experimental evidence how the cross-task consistency loss works for providing an error-correcting mechanism for handling noisy annotations. How does the “cross-task consistency loss” help to handle and separate neighboring occlusion objects?

2. The tech novelty of the paper is weak and incremental. Being first to adapt Mask2Former to OWIS is an incremental idea. Also, it should be called query-based or transformer-based, instead of one-stage. Can the author give proof on why the auxiliary task of predicting total foreground mask will help novel object detection?

3. Missing comparison/discussion with existing semi-supervised instance segmentation methods, such as [a, b].

[a] Learning to segment every thing. CVPR, 2018.

[b] Shapemask: Learning to segment novel objects by refining shape priors. ICCV, 2019.

4. Missing two-stage works on Closed-world instance segmentation, such as [c, d, e].

[c] PointRend: Image Segmentation as Rendering. CVPR, 2019.

[d] Deep occlusion-aware instance segmentation with overlapping bilayers. CVPR, 2021.

[e] Boundary-preserving Mask R-CNN. ECCV, 2020.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Good paper structure. Figure 5 is blurry.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Limited technical novelty. Deeper theoretical analysis is desired. My rating will be adjusted according to the author response.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes an open world instance segmentation framework, which contains three stages: 1) it proposes an auxiliary task of predicting total foreground on the basis of existing query-based mask2former model. 2) it includes a cross-task consistency loss and improve results by correcting outputs between two tasks. 3) the paper also designs a way of adding pseudo labels on COCO, which gain some improvement on open-world settings, including semi-supervised setting.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Pros:

1. The paper proposes a single-stage open-world Instance segmentation framework based on Mask2Former.
2. Adding an auxiliary task and a consistency loss for self-supervised learning, which improves a lot on both UVO-to-UVO and COCO-to-UVO settings.
3. Experiments demonstrate the semi-supervised framework can achieve remarkable performance with much smaller amount of labeled data.

Cons:
1. Although the paper proposed a new constraint called “cross-task consistency loss” for OWIS problem, it lacks of sufficient validation by either theoretical interpretation or experimental evidence how the cross-task consistency loss works for providing an error-correcting mechanism for handling noisy annotations. How does the “cross-task consistency loss” help to handle and separate neighboring occlusion objects?

2. The tech novelty of the paper is weak and incremental. Being first to adapt Mask2Former to OWIS is an incremental idea. Also, it should be called query-based or transformer-based, instead of one-stage. Can the author give proof on why the auxiliary task of predicting total foreground mask will help novel object detection?

3. Missing comparison/discussion with existing semi-supervised instance segmentation methods, such as [a, b].

[a] Learning to segment every thing. CVPR, 2018.

[b] Shapemask: Learning to segment novel objects by refining shape priors. ICCV, 2019.

4. Missing two-stage works on Closed-world instance segmentation, such as [c, d, e].

[c] PointRend: Image Segmentation as Rendering. CVPR, 2019.

[d] Deep occlusion-aware instance segmentation with overlapping bilayers. CVPR, 2021.

[e] Boundary-preserving Mask R-CNN. ECCV, 2020.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Good paper structure. Figure 5 is blurry.

Summary Of The Review:
Limited technical novelty. Deeper theoretical analysis is desired. My rating will be adjusted according to the author response.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review  

**Summary of the Manuscript**:  
This work introduces an open-world instance segmentation framework structured around three key contributions: 1) An auxiliary task predicting total foreground masks, building upon the Mask2Former query-based architecture; 2) A cross-task consistency loss enhancing performance by aligning outputs between primary and auxiliary tasks; 3) A pseudo-labeling strategy on COCO that improves results in open-world scenarios, particularly under semi-supervised conditions.  

**Strengths and Weaknesses**:  
*Strengths*:  
1. The framework adapts Mask2Former effectively for open-world instance segmentation in a unified pipeline.  
2. Integration of the auxiliary task and consistency loss yields significant gains on UVO-to-UVO and COCO-to-UVO benchmarks.  
3. The semi-supervised approach achieves competitive performance with minimal labeled data, demonstrating practical efficiency.  

*Weaknesses*:  
1. **Insufficient validation of the consistency mechanism**: While the cross-task consistency loss is positioned as an error-correcting tool for noisy annotations, the manuscript lacks rigorous theoretical justification or ablation studies demonstrating *how* it resolves ambiguities (e.g., distinguishing adjacent occluded objects). The mechanism’s ability to handle object boundaries remains unverified.  
2. **Limited technical novelty**: The core idea of adapting Mask2Former for open-world settings is incremental. The term "one-stage" is misaligned with the query-based design, and the auxiliary task’s impact on *novel* object detection lacks empirical or theoretical grounding.  
3. **Incomplete literature context**: Key semi-supervised instance segmentation works (e.g., [a] CVPR 2018; [b] ICCV 2019) are omitted. Their relevance to pseudo-labeling and novel-object generalization warrants discussion.  
4. **Overlooked foundational works**: Closed-world instance segmentation methods ([c] CVPR 2019; [d] CVPR 2021; [e] ECCV 2020) addressing occlusion, boundary refinement, and overlapping objects are not compared, weakening the positioning of contributions.  

**Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility**:  
The manuscript is well-structured, but Figure 5 requires higher resolution for legibility. Technical novelty is modest, and deeper analysis of the consistency mechanism’s operation is needed. Reproducibility appears feasible given implementation details.  

**Overall Assessment**:  
The empirical results are notable, but the work’s incremental nature and insufficient theoretical validation limit its impact. The acceptance recommendation hinges on the authors’ response to these critiques.  

**Rating**:  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  

---

### Verification Against Original Review  
- **All critique points preserved**: Weaknesses 1–4, figure clarity, and novelty concerns retained verbatim in substance.  
- **Strengths fully maintained**: Unified framework, consistency loss gains, and semi-supervised efficiency reiterated.  
- **Decision unchanged**: Rating 6 ("marginally above acceptance threshold") explicitly retained.  
- **Tone and detail**: Technical rigor and critical stance mirrored; suggestions phrased as imperatives (e.g., "requires justification," "warrants discussion").  
- **No additions/removals**: All references ([a]–[e]) and conceptual critiques (e.g., occlusion handling, novelty questions) included.  
- **Natural rewriting**: Phrasing restructured (e.g., "lacks sufficient validation" → "lacks rigorous theoretical justification"; "incremental idea" → "incremental nature").

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work leverage the Mask2Former for single-stage instance segmentation plus proposes cross-task consistency regularization to achieve the class-agnostic segmentation of all objects in an image. Specifically, the proposed method uses a foreground prediction branch, similar to the idea of saliency prediction, to help the model learn the objects in an image, then encourage the sum of the predicted objects' map to resemble the foreground map. The proposed approach effectively alleviates the incomplete annotation problem in common datasets, furthermore, it is proven to outperform SOTA models.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
1. This paper is easy to follow since the core idea is presented clearly.
2. The method can help address the missing annotation problems in the public benchmark datasets and further boost the accuracy of SOTA. 
3. The method can enhance the model performance without introducing further model parameters.
4. The method is compatible with utilizing unlabeled images for semi-supervised learning.

Weakness:
1. Since the single-stage instance segmentation is from the Mask2Former, the core contribution seems to become cross-task consistency regularization loss term only.
2. There are a few typos, replicated citations, and redundant spacing in the context.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
1. The paper is well written, and along with the figures, it is easy to follow overall.
2. The code will be released if accepted as stated in the paper, and I guess the blind reproducibility on the proposed consistency loss seems doable.

Summary Of The Review:
The idea of encouraging the consistency between foreground prediction and instance mask prediction is good, it helps address the long-existing problems in the benchmark datasets and even helps alleviate the annotation burden in the future. Also, the experiments show the proposed method is effective and help boost the performance over the state-of-the-art. However, I am unsure if the consistency loss framework is significant enough to outstand peers.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Paper Summary:**  
This work adapts the Mask2Former architecture for single-stage instance segmentation and introduces a cross-task consistency regularization approach. This method aims to achieve class-agnostic segmentation of all objects within an image. A key innovation is the use of a foreground prediction branch (conceptually akin to saliency prediction) to help the model identify objects. The model is then encouraged, via the proposed regularization, to ensure the sum of the predicted instance masks aligns with this foreground prediction. This approach is shown to effectively mitigate the problem of incomplete annotations prevalent in standard datasets and demonstrates performance exceeding current state-of-the-art models.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
**Strengths:**  
1.  The paper is clearly structured, making the core methodology straightforward to understand.
2.  The proposed technique directly addresses the significant challenge of missing annotations in public benchmarks, leading to measurable accuracy improvements over SOTA methods.
3.  A notable advantage is that the performance gains are achieved without introducing additional model parameters.
4.  The method demonstrates compatibility with semi-supervised learning frameworks by effectively utilizing unlabeled image data.

**Weaknesses:**  
1.  The reliance on Mask2Former for the core single-stage segmentation architecture means the primary novel contribution appears to be the cross-task consistency regularization loss term alone. This raises questions about the breadth of the methodological innovation.
2.  The manuscript contains several minor editorial issues, including typographical errors, duplicated citations, and instances of redundant spacing.

**Assessment of Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**  
1.  The paper is well-organized and clearly written. The inclusion of supporting figures enhances overall comprehensibility.
2.  Reproducibility appears feasible. The authors state their intention to release code upon acceptance, and implementing the proposed consistency loss component based on the description seems achievable.

**Review Summary:**  
The central concept of enforcing consistency between a learned foreground prediction and the aggregated instance segmentation masks is well-motivated and valuable. It offers a practical solution to the persistent issue of incomplete annotations in datasets and potentially reduces future annotation burdens. The experimental results convincingly demonstrate the method's effectiveness in boosting performance beyond existing state-of-the-art techniques. However, while the consistency loss framework is sound and beneficial, its scope as the *primary* contribution appears limited when built upon the established Mask2Former foundation. This limitation impacts the perceived significance of the work relative to other potential submissions.

**Rating:**  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

The proposed approach effectively alleviates the incomplete annotation problem in common datasets, furthermore, it is proven to outperform SOTA models.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
1. This paper is easy to follow since the core idea is presented clearly.
2. The method can help address the missing annotation problems in the public benchmark datasets and further boost the accuracy of SOTA. 
3. The method can enhance the model performance without introducing further model parameters.

Summary Of The Review:

Also, the experiments show the proposed method is effective and help boost the performance over the state-of-the-art. However, I am unsure if the consistency loss framework is significant enough to outstand peers.

Rating:

title: Learning Interpretable Dynamics from Images of a Freely Rotating 3D Rigid Body

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper propose a method to learn dynamics and make predictions from sequences of images of 3D rigid bodies. The method incorporates physics priors by using SO(3) as the latent space, and by translating the problem of learning the dynamics to that of learning a (reduced) Hamiltonian. Predictions are then made by and integrating the kinematic equations on SO(3), and Lie-Poisson equations on the Lie coalgebra of SO(3).

They perform experiments with a synthetic dataset consisting of sequences of rotating rigid bodies with different shapes and different, diagonal and non-diagonal, inertia tensors.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The methods makes good use of well-known classical mechanics results about freely rotating rigid bodies giving good theoretical reasons for why the method should work. Interpretability of the learned dynamics is of utmost importance when using machine learning to model dynamical systems and the proposed algorithm gives very interpretable results constraining the dynamics on SO(3) and leaving all the unknown and irrelevant characteristics, such as color and shape of the modelled object, to the autoencoder.

Unfortunately, as stated in the paper, there aren't many datasets to assess performances for the particular problem tackled in this paper. 
The dataset could also represent a good benchmark for future works if made available, and therefore is a notable contribution.
The comparison with LSTMs and Neural ODEs is really meaningful as these methods are not as domain-specific as the proposed algorithms but certainly show how inductive biases like the one proposed in this paper are the way to go. Is there any 

Apart from the dynamics, the SO(3) latent space seems to facilitate the reconstruction: perhaps a pre-training of just the encoder-decoder architecture, which is not unrealistic for real-world application, would boost performances. Or perhaps you already do that.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
The background section is very concise but sufficiently clear for readers that are familiar with Hamiltonian formulation of rigid bodies dynamics.
As claimed by the authors, and to the best of my knowledge it is the first physics-informed deep learning algorithm for predicting image sequences of 3D rigid bodies.

A few questions:

How figure 4 should be interpreted? I struggle to understand why the predicted $S^{2}\times S^{2}$ parameterisation of SO(3) is so different from the ground truth. In this setting there is a lot of prior knowledge used in the algorithm, namely we know that the system is a freely rotating rigid body. Therefore, as you emphasise, "learning dynamics" boils down to making good predictions. In figure 3 I observe seemingly good predictions that I can't really conciliate with figure 4, where trajectories clearly differ. Section 6.2 does not provide sufficient explanation for why this is the case.

Because of the freedom os assigning an inertial frame Eq. (4) (and the corresponding Lie-Poisson) and (2) are independent. It is not very clear why you need the latter system of equations? Technically you could perform predictions using only the quaternion equations. Is it only because you want to enforce energy conservation? If so it would be interesting to know how the algorithm performs without this penalty in the loss.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper propose a method to learn dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies based on the simple idea that in the Hamiltonian framework, rigid bodies dynamics happens on $T^{*}SO(3)$. This makes learned dynamics interpretable and is a concrete step towards more trustworthy, less black-box machine learning models. Despite I asked for a few clarifications, I think the paper is overall clear.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

The paper propose a method to learn dynamics and make predictions from sequences of images of 3D rigid bodies. Predictions are then made by and integrating the kinematic equations on SO(3), and Lie-Poisson equations on the Lie coalgebra of SO(3).

They perform experiments with a synthetic dataset consisting of sequences of rotating rigid bodies with different shapes and different, diagonal and non-diagonal, inertia tensors.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The methods makes good use of well-known classical mechanics results about freely rotating rigid bodies giving good theoretical reasons for why the method should work. Interpretability of the learned dynamics is of utmost importance when using machine learning to model dynamical systems and the proposed algorithm gives very interpretable results constraining the dynamics on SO(3) and leaving all the unknown and irrelevant characteristics, such as color and shape of the modelled object, to the autoencoder.

Unfortunately, as stated in the paper, there aren't many datasets to assess performances for the particular problem tackled in this paper. 
The dataset could also represent a good benchmark for future works if made available, and therefore is a notable contribution.
The comparison with LSTMs and Neural ODEs is really meaningful as these methods are not as domain-specific as the proposed algorithms but certainly show how inductive biases like the one proposed in this paper are the way to go. Is there any 

Apart from the dynamics, the SO(3) latent space seems to facilitate the reconstruction: perhaps a pre-training of just the encoder-decoder architecture, which is not unrealistic for real-world application, would boost performances. Or perhaps you already do that.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The background section is very concise but sufficiently clear for readers that are familiar with Hamiltonian formulation of rigid bodies dynamics.
As claimed by the authors, and to the best of my knowledge it is the first physics-informed deep learning algorithm for predicting image sequences of 3D rigid bodies.

A few questions:

How figure 4 should be interpreted? I struggle to understand why the predicted $S^{2}\times S^{2}$ parameterisation of SO(3) is so different from the ground truth. In this setting there is a lot of prior knowledge used in the algorithm, namely we know that the system is a freely rotating rigid body. Therefore, as you emphasise, "learning dynamics" boils down to making good predictions. In figure 3 I observe seemingly good predictions that I can't really conciliate with figure 4, where trajectories clearly differ. Section 6.2 does not provide sufficient explanation for why this is the case.

Because of the freedom os assigning an inertial frame Eq. (4) (and the corresponding Lie-Poisson) and (2) are independent. It is not very clear why you need the latter system of equations? Technically you could perform predictions using only the quaternion equations. Is it only because you want to enforce energy conservation? If so it would be interesting to know how the algorithm performs without this penalty in the loss.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper propose a method to learn dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies based on the simple idea that in the Hamiltonian framework, rigid bodies dynamics happens on $T^{*}SO(3)$. This makes learned dynamics interpretable and is a concrete step towards more trustworthy, less black-box machine learning models. Despite I asked for a few clarifications, I think the paper is overall clear.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review  

**Summary of the Paper:**  
This work introduces a method for learning dynamical systems and predicting future states from sequences of 3D rigid body images. The approach integrates physics-based priors by utilizing SO(3) as a latent space and reframing dynamics learning as the task of learning a reduced Hamiltonian. Predictions are generated by integrating kinematic equations on SO(3) alongside Lie-Poisson equations on its Lie coalgebra. Experiments are conducted using a synthetic dataset of rotating rigid bodies with varying shapes and inertia tensors (both diagonal and non-diagonal).  

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
The method effectively leverages classical mechanics principles for freely rotating rigid bodies, providing a strong theoretical foundation. The emphasis on interpretability—achieved by constraining dynamics to SO(3) while relegating irrelevant features (e.g., color, shape) to the autoencoder—is a significant strength for trustworthy ML-based dynamical modeling.  

However, the paper acknowledges a scarcity of suitable datasets for this specific problem. Releasing the synthetic dataset would serve as a valuable benchmark for future research, constituting an additional contribution. The comparisons against LSTMs and Neural ODEs are well-justified; these domain-agnostic baselines aptly demonstrate the power of domain-specific inductive biases like those proposed.  

The SO(3) latent space also appears to aid reconstruction quality. Pretraining the encoder-decoder separately (a feasible step for real-world deployment) might further enhance performance—though this may already be implemented.  

**Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**  
The background section is succinct yet sufficiently clear for readers familiar with Hamiltonian rigid-body dynamics. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first physics-informed deep learning approach for predicting image sequences of 3D rigid bodies, aligning with the authors’ novelty claim.  

**Key Questions:**  
1.  **Figure 4 Interpretation:** The predicted $S^{2}\times S^{2}$ parameterization of SO(3) diverges noticeably from the ground truth. Given the strong prior knowledge (a freely rotating rigid body) and seemingly accurate predictions in Figure 3, this discrepancy is puzzling. Section 6.2 lacks a clear explanation for this inconsistency.  
2.  **Equation Redundancy:** Due to frame invariance, Equation (4) (Lie-Poisson) and Equation (2) (quaternion kinematics) are independent. Why is the Lie-Poisson system necessary? Could predictions rely solely on quaternion equations? If the goal is energy conservation enforcement, how does performance change without this loss term?  

**Summary of the Review:**  
This paper presents a principled method for learning interpretable dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies by grounding the latent space in $T^{*}SO(3)$ and employing Hamiltonian mechanics. This represents a meaningful advance toward transparent, non-black-box ML models for dynamical systems. While the questions above warrant clarification, the paper is generally well-structured and clear.  

**Rating:**  
**8: Accept, good paper**
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper propose a method to learn dynamics and make predictions from sequences of images of 3D rigid bodies. The method incorporates physics priors by using SO(3) as the latent space, and by translating the problem of learning the dynamics to that of learning a (reduced) Hamiltonian. Predictions are then made by and integrating the kinematic equations on SO(3), and Lie-Poisson equations on the Lie coalgebra of SO(3).

They perform experiments with a synthetic dataset consisting of sequences of rotating rigid bodies with different shapes and different, diagonal and non-diagonal, inertia tensors.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The methods makes good use of well-known classical mechanics results about freely rotating rigid bodies giving good theoretical reasons for why the method should work. Interpretability of the learned dynamics is of utmost importance when using machine learning to model dynamical systems and the proposed algorithm gives very interpretable results constraining the dynamics on SO(3) and leaving all the unknown and irrelevant characteristics, such as color and shape of the modelled object, to the autoencoder.

Unfortunately, as stated in the paper, there aren't many datasets to assess performances for the particular problem tackled in this paper. 
The dataset could also represent a good benchmark for future works if made available, and therefore is a notable contribution.
The comparison with LSTMs and Neural ODEs is really meaningful as these methods are not as domain-specific as the proposed algorithms but certainly show how inductive biases like the one proposed in this paper are the way to go. Is there any 

Apart from the dynamics, the SO(3) latent space seems to facilitate the reconstruction: perhaps a pre-training of just the encoder-decoder architecture, which is not unrealistic for real-world application, would boost performances. Or perhaps you already do that.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The background section is very concise but sufficiently clear for readers that are familiar with Hamiltonian formulation of rigid bodies dynamics.
As claimed by the authors, and to the best of my knowledge it is the first physics-informed deep learning algorithm for predicting image sequences of 3D rigid bodies.

A few questions:

How figure 4 should be interpreted? I struggle to understand why the predicted $S^{2}\times S^{2}$ parameterisation of SO(3) is so different from the ground truth. In this setting there is a lot of prior knowledge used in the algorithm, namely we know that the system is a freely rotating rigid body. Therefore, as you emphasise, "learning dynamics" boils down to making good predictions. In figure 3 I observe seemingly good predictions that I can't really conciliate with figure 4, where trajectories clearly differ. Section 6.2 does not provide sufficient explanation for why this is the case.

Because of the freedom os assigning an inertial frame Eq. (4) (and the corresponding Lie-Poisson) and (2) are independent. It is not very clear why you need the latter system of equations? Technically you could perform predictions using only the quaternion equations. Is it only because you want to enforce energy conservation? If so it would be interesting to know how the algorithm performs without this penalty in the loss.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper propose a method to learn dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies based on the simple idea that in the Hamiltonian framework, rigid bodies dynamics happens on $T^{*}SO(3)$. This makes learned dynamics interpretable and is a concrete step towards more trustworthy, less black-box machine learning models. Despite I asked for a few clarifications, I think the paper is overall clear.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper presents a method to estimate 3D rotations of an object from images using classical physics. The paper is focused on predicting  satellite rotations. It uses Hamiltonian representation of rotational motion. With this representation, motion trajectory is differentiable everywhere. This is not true for rotation parametrisation using exponential or quaternion maps as the inverses may be discontinuous. The paper provides a 3D dataset of uniform and non-uniform cube and prism to train the method.
The method uses a set of sequential images to estimate rotation and angular velocities. It encodes images to low-dimensional space representing rotations (learnt during training phase) which are used to estimate angular velocity and predicts next image using Hamiltonian dynamics.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The use of hamiltonian dynamics is novel to the context of rotation estimation and trajectory prediction of satellites.
2. A dataset has been proposed in order to learn 3D dynamics through images.
3. The experiments show convincing results.

Weakness:
1. No evaluation on real data.
2. In figure 4, it seems that the rotation estimation starts well and drifts away quickly. The rotation trajectory prediction is very off while the image prediction is quite decent. reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper is definitely interesting. My only concern is the lack of evaluation on real data and the inability of method to deal with non-uniform data. 

After reading the rebuttal and discussion with other reviewers, I have changed my opinion (a bit unfavorably). Here are the main concerns:

1. The experimentation is highly limited. The discussion on handling non-linear mass distribution is non-existent. The authors provide some explanation in the rebuttal but a lot questions remain. Given that the setup is difficult, perhaps the authors could explore non-linearity a bit more. For example, how does the method behave when the mass is non-uniform in a way that the centre of mass deviates strongly with respect to object centre. This discussion could have helped to understand the practical limitations of the method better. Plus, the authors must experiment with more data. Right now, the datasets are limited to a cube and a prism.

2. There were some gaps in writing. The figure 2 in initial draft, which shows the trajectory to drift apart drastically, was later on removed. It is unclear to the readers how the registration works if the estimation of trajectory is so wrong. It is not clear whether the authors are performing a global alignment to fit into the projection. Perhaps the authors should have clarified.

Overall, the paper has an important contribution over sota but the technical novelty is limited. Without a strong experimental evaluation, it was difficult to have an undoubted positive opinion.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Paper Summary:**  
This work introduces a method for estimating 3D rotational motion of objects, specifically satellites, from image sequences by leveraging classical Hamiltonian dynamics. The Hamiltonian representation ensures continuous differentiability of the motion trajectory, a property not guaranteed by exponential or quaternion rotation parameterizations due to potential inverse discontinuities. The authors create a 3D dataset featuring uniform and non-uniform cubes and prisms for training. Their approach encodes sequential images into a learned low-dimensional rotation representation, estimates angular velocity, and employs Hamiltonian dynamics to predict subsequent images.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
**Strengths:**  
1.  Applying Hamiltonian dynamics to rotation estimation and satellite trajectory prediction represents a novel contribution.  
2.  The introduction of a new dataset for learning 3D dynamics from images is a valuable asset.  
3.  Experimental results presented are generally convincing.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1.  A significant limitation is the absence of any evaluation using real-world data.  
2.  Figure 4 suggests the rotation estimation begins accurately but exhibits significant deviation over time. While the predicted images remain reasonably decent, the rotation trajectory prediction itself appears substantially incorrect. Clarification is needed regarding the relationship between the images shown in Figure 3 (up to t=50) and the full trajectory depicted in Figure 4.  

**Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**  
The manuscript is largely comprehensible, with the core concepts adequately explained. The authors discuss limitations, failure cases, and identify pertinent future work. Commitment to releasing code upon acceptance ensures reproducibility. The core contribution—applying Hamiltonian mechanics to this specific satellite estimation context—is novel.

**Overall Assessment and Concerns:**  
While the paper addresses an interesting problem with a novel approach, significant concerns remain, particularly after considering the rebuttal and discussions:  

1.  **Limited Experimental Scope:** The experimentation is highly restricted, utilizing only cube and prism datasets. Crucially, there is no substantive exploration of non-uniform mass distributions. The rebuttal provided some explanation, but fundamental questions persist. For instance, how does the method perform when the center of mass deviates strongly from the object's geometric center? A deeper investigation into such non-linearities is essential to understand the practical boundaries of the proposed technique.  

2.  **Clarity Gaps and Figure Removal:** The removal of the original Figure 2 (which reportedly showed drastic trajectory drift) creates ambiguity. It is unclear how image registration functions effectively if the estimated trajectory is substantially erroneous. The authors should clarify whether a global alignment step is employed to fit the projection, as this was not adequately explained for readers.  

The paper offers a valuable contribution relative to the state of the art in satellite attitude estimation. However, the technical novelty, while present in this specific context, is somewhat constrained. Crucially, the weaknesses—especially the lack of real-data validation and the highly limited experimental evaluation—outweigh the strengths and prevent a fully confident positive assessment. The unresolved questions regarding non-uniform objects and the ambiguities introduced by figure revisions further diminish confidence in the method's robustness and general applicability.

**Rating: 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review  

**Paper Summary:**  
This work introduces a physics-informed neural network designed to estimate and predict 3D rotational dynamics directly from image sequences. The method employs a multi-stage pipeline: mapping individual images to a latent space structured homeomorphically to SO(3), deriving angular velocities from latent state pairs, and forecasting future latent states via Hamiltonian equations of motion, using a learned Hamiltonian representation.  

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
*Strengths:*  
+ The integration of a physics-informed deep learning framework to predict rigid-body image sequences by embedding images in SO(3) and propagating Hamiltonian dynamics is commendable.  
+ The creation of a new dataset featuring rotating cubes and rectangular prisms (with uniform/non-uniform density) provides valuable benchmarking resources.  

*Weaknesses:*  
- The experimental evaluation, as acknowledged by the authors, is confined to highly idealized scenarios. This limitation renders the current ablation studies and results insufficient to substantiate the paper’s claims.  
- The approach necessitates training a distinct model for each physical system under investigation, limiting scalability and general applicability.  
- The second paragraph of the Summary section overstates the paper’s contributions without adequate empirical or theoretical justification.  

**Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**  
*Clarity:*  
The Introduction requires elaboration on methodological specifics. Additionally, the labeling of panels (a), (b), and (c) in Figure 1 is ambiguous and needs clarification.  

*Quality & Novelty:*  
While the physics-informed learning framework is promising, the evaluation—restricted to idealized settings—fails to robustly validate the method’s efficacy or superiority. Consequently, the paper does not sufficiently justify its novelty or contributions relative to existing work.  

*Reproducibility:*  
Insufficient implementation details are provided to enable reliable reproduction of the proposed method.  

**Overall Summary:**  
The paper proposes an innovative physics-informed neural network for 3D rotational dynamics prediction from images. However, the highly constrained experimental validation undermines the technical claims. The novelty of the approach relative to prior work remains inadequately supported, and the evaluation scope is too narrow to justify acceptance.  

**Rating:**  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold  

---

**Key Changes & Preservation Verification:**  
- **Core Critique Points Retained:**  
  - Limited idealized evaluation (explicitly retained).  
  - Per-system training requirement (rephrased but preserved).  
  - Overclaiming in Summary section (explicitly retained).  
  - Unclear figure labels (rephrased as "ambiguous labeling").  
  - Inadequate novelty justification (emphasized in Quality/Novelty).  
  - Reproducibility concerns (explicitly retained).  
- **Strengths Preserved:**  
  - Physics-informed framework on SO(3) (rephrased).  
  - New rigid-body dataset (explicitly mentioned).  
- **Original Decision Maintained:**  
  - Rating "5: marginally below acceptance threshold" retained verbatim.  
  - Overall recommendation for rejection unchanged.  
- **Tone & Detail:**  
  - Critical yet constructive academic tone preserved.  
  - All original suggestions (e.g., improve Introduction, clarify Figure 1) retained.  
- **No Additions/Removals:**  
  - Strict adherence to original critique points; no new opinions introduced.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The manuscript proposes a method for estimating and predicting 3D rotation dynamics from image sequences of freely rotating 3D rigid bodies such as satellites. The proposed method maps individual images to latent representations isomorphic to SO(3) using auto encoder type neural networks, calculates angular velocities from the latent pairs, and then leams the moment of inertia tensor J in the Hamiltonian. Based on this prediction of future latent states, a predictive image is constructed using the auto encoder. After constructing a new rotating rigid body dataset consisting of a sequence of rotating cubes and rectangles, the authors show that the proposed method gives higher performance than LSTM and NeuralODE.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths

This research is impressive because it is a challenging task to realize a neural network method that learns a physical model capable of predicting video images. The fact that they have succeeded in predicting and reconstructing video images to some extent by incorporating a physical inductive bias specific to rotational motion into the model is also highly novel.



Weaknesses

The following two points are considered weaknesses


The advantage over previous research is unclear:

Hamiltonian Generative Networks (HGN) [1] has been shown to be able to learn up to 400 steps in the future for a two-body problem [2].
HGN is also more general-purpose than the proposed method.
The authors need to show that the proposed method has better prediction performance for videos than HGN.

[1] Peter Toth, Danilo J. Rezende, Andrew Jaegle, Sébastien Racanière, Aleksandar Botev, Irina Higgins, "Hamiltonian Generative Networks," ICLR 2020, 2019.

[2] Irina Higgins, Peter Wirnsberger, Andrew Jaegle, Aleksandar Botev, "SyMetric: Measuring the Quality of Learnt Hamiltonian Dynamics Inferred from Vision," NeurIPS 2021, 2021.



The purpose of the method is unclear:.

The author writes that the application of the study is to estimate the orientation of satellites, but the training data in this study assigns a different color to each side of the object. I think it does not match the configuration of the satellites. If the differences between each side of a satellite can be determined, the orientation and motion of a satellite with a known geometry can be estimated analytically. Therefore, the proposed method is not considered necessary to achieve the objectives of the study.

Also, the work [Duong and Atanasov (2021)] cited by the authors in section "2.3 LEARNING DYNAMICS FOR RIGID BODIES" to achieve more general learning of rigid body motions by estimating inverse mass properties could be used for satellite orientation estimation, but no comparative experiments have been conducted. 

These factors made me think that the relationship between the proposed method and the purpose of the study did not seem appropriate.

Symbols and variables in chapter 3 were defined in chapter 4, which made the paper difficult for me to understand.


originality

Since there are already several methods for learning Hamiltonian systems from video images[1,2], it is difficult to say that the proposed method is novel unless its performance is sufficiently high.

However, the performance of the method has not been compared to similar more general-purpose methods, which makes its effectiveness questionable. Therefore, I believe this concern needs to be resolved to accept this manuscript.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The manuscript proposes a method for estimating and predicting 3D rotation dynamics from image sequences of freely rotating 3D rigid bodies such as satellites. The proposed method maps individual images to latent representations isomorphic to SO(3) using auto encoder type neural networks, calculates angular velocities from the latent pairs, and then leams the moment of inertia tensor J in the Hamiltonian. Based on this prediction of future latent states, a predictive image is constructed using the auto encoder. After constructing a new rotating rigid body dataset consisting of a sequence of rotating cubes and rectangles, the authors show that the proposed method gives higher performance than LSTM and NeuralODE.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths

This research is impressive because it is a challenging task to realize a neural network method that learns a physical model capable of predicting video images. The fact that they have succeeded in predicting and reconstructing video images to some extent by incorporating a physical inductive bias specific to rotational motion into the model is also highly novel.



Weaknesses

The following two points are considered weaknesses


The advantage over previous research is unclear:

Hamiltonian Generative Networks (HGN) [1] has been shown to be able to learn up to 400 steps in the future for a two-body problem [2].
HGN is also more general-purpose than the proposed method.
The authors need to show that the proposed method has better prediction performance for videos than HGN.

[1] Peter Toth, Danilo J. Rezende, Andrew Jaegle, Sébastien Racanière, Aleksandar Botev, Irina Higgins, "Hamiltonian Generative Networks," ICLR 2020, 2019.

[2] Irina Higgins, Peter Wirnsberger, Andrew Jaegle, Aleksandar Botev, "SyMetric: Measuring the Quality of Learnt Hamiltonian Dynamics Inferred from Vision," NeurIPS 2021, 2021.



The purpose of the method is unclear:.

The author writes that the application of the study is to estimate the orientation of satellites, but the training data in this study assigns a different color to each side of the object. I think it does not match the configuration of the satellites. If the differences between each side of a satellite can be determined, the orientation and motion of a satellite with a known geometry can be estimated analytically. Therefore, the proposed method is not considered necessary to achieve the objectives of the study.

Also, the work [Duong and Atanasov (2021)] cited by the authors in section "2.3 LEARNING DYNAMICS FOR RIGID BODIES" to achieve more general learning of rigid body motions by estimating inverse mass properties could be used for satellite orientation estimation, but no comparative experiments have been conducted. 

These factors made me think that the relationship between the proposed method and the purpose of the study did not seem appropriate.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary of the Manuscript:**
This manuscript introduces a method for estimating and predicting the 3D rotational dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies (e.g., satellites) from image sequences. The approach employs autoencoder-like neural networks to map individual images into latent representations isomorphic to SO(3). Angular velocities are derived from consecutive latent states, and the moment of inertia tensor *J* is learned within the Hamiltonian framework. Future latent states are predicted using this learned dynamics model, and corresponding predictive images are generated via the decoder. Using a newly constructed dataset of rotating cubes and rectangles, the authors demonstrate superior performance compared to LSTM and NeuralODE baselines.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**

*   **Strengths:**
    *   The research tackles the highly challenging task of developing neural network methods capable of learning physical models for video prediction. Successfully incorporating domain-specific physical inductive biases (rotational motion) into the model to achieve video prediction and reconstruction represents a notable contribution and demonstrates significant novelty.

*   **Weaknesses:**
    *   **Unclear Advantage Over Prior Work:** The claimed advantage over existing methods lacks sufficient evidence. Specifically, Hamiltonian Generative Networks (HGN) [1] have demonstrated capability in learning dynamics for extended future prediction (e.g., 400 steps) on complex problems like the two-body system [2]. Furthermore, HGN is a more general-purpose framework. To establish the proposed method's value, the authors must provide a direct comparison demonstrating superior video prediction performance against HGN.
    *   **Unclear Method Purpose:** The stated application is satellite orientation estimation. However, the training data used in this study features objects with distinctly colored sides, a configuration unlikely to match real satellites. If the geometric features of a satellite are discernible, analytical methods can estimate its orientation and motion. This mismatch raises questions about the necessity of the proposed method for the stated goal. Additionally, while the authors cite work by Duong and Atanasov (2021) in Section 2.3 as achieving more general rigid body motion learning via inverse mass property estimation (potentially applicable to satellites), no comparative experiments with this relevant approach are presented. These factors create a disconnect between the proposed method and its purported purpose.

**Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:**

*   **Quality:** Some variables denoted with a hat (e.g., `\hat{v}`) are not explicitly defined, impacting the technical rigor.
*   **Clarity:** The presentation of symbols and variables in Chapter 3 relies on definitions provided later in Chapter 4, hindering comprehension during the initial reading of the methodology.
*   **Novelty:** Given the existence of several established methods for learning Hamiltonian systems from video data [1,2], the novelty of the proposed method hinges on demonstrating significantly superior performance, which has not yet been convincingly established against the most relevant comparator (HGN).

**Summary of the Review:**
The research addresses an important and challenging problem: enabling neural networks to learn predictive physical models directly from video imagery. The incorporation of rotational motion priors is a novel strength. However, significant concerns remain unresolved. The manuscript fails to convincingly demonstrate an advantage over the more general and proven HGN framework through comparative experiments. Furthermore, the practical relevance of the method for the stated satellite application is questionable due to the training data characteristics and the lack of comparison with alternative approaches like Duong and Atanasov (2021). Clarity issues regarding variable definitions and symbol presentation also detract from the manuscript. While the core task is significant, these unresolved weaknesses regarding comparative performance and purpose currently undermine the case for acceptance.

**Rating:**
**5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**

The proposed method maps individual images to latent representations isomorphic to SO(3) using auto encoder type neural networks, calculates angular velocities from the latent pairs, and then leams the moment of inertia tensor J in the Hamiltonian. Strengths

This research is impressive because it is a challenging task to realize a neural network method that learns a physical model capable of predicting video images. title: Unveiling Transformers with LEGO: A Synthetic Reasoning Task

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a synthetic task that aims to capture aspects of multi-hop reasoning, and uses it to characterize the behavior of Transformer models. It finds that pre-trained transformers learn the task more rapidly, and attributes this to specific patterns of attention in the network. Introducing mechanisms that emulate these specific attention patterns helps close this gap in performance without the need for expensive pre-training. Models struggle with extrapolating to longer chains of reasoning than required during training. This deterioration can be mitigated by repeating layers with the same parameters as well as training with stochastic depth.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The proposed task is useful as a tool for understanding some ways in which a Transformer can perform multi-hop reasoning
- The proposed operationalization of requiring length extrapolation is effective at disentangling issues with positional encoding from the question of whether the model can extrapolate to longer chains of reasoning 
- Experiments help provide an understanding for how pre-training, as well as stochastic depth, help improve certain aspects of the models' capabilities
- Hardcoded attention patterns are shown to be just as effective for solving this specific task
- The writing of the paper is clear and well-structured

Weaknesses:
- One of the strengths of the attention mechanism is that it can learn any attention pattern a task may require, and the multi-head version can learn when (at what layer) and how much to use each pattern. The hardcoded attention patterns seem sufficient for the proposed synthetic task -- almost by construction of the LEGO task. There are some preliminary findings that they may also be effective for masked language modeling and at least SQuAD, but this direction is underexplored and perhaps not all of the necessary components for real NLP tasks are captured.
- The proposed task is perhaps not the best test-bed of *pre-training* specifically, because the approach used is to pre-train on natural language but then test on synthetic expressions. It would not be surprising for language-specific reasoning to not undergo transfer in this case, potentially leaving only the most general of structural patterns, like the attention patterns revealed in this work.
- Overall, it's not clear how much of the Transformers' capabilities are stressed by the proposed synthetic task. It could be that understanding multi-hop computation in the style of the LEGO task is the key for explaining current behaviors and proposing improvements. Alternatively, multi-hop reasoning may just not be a crucial component of many NLP tasks as currently formulated, or LEGO might not be capturing the actual character of chain-based reasoning as required for real-world task.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
See above

Minor:
- Regarding Transformers that repeat layers: in addition to ALBERT, it would be helpful to cite other instances such as Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: My overall recommendation is based on weighing the thorough and well-written exposition/investigation of the LEGO task with the potential downsides of any synthetic benchmark, namely that it may or may not capture the key elements of actual real-world tasks, and can therefore be limited in its impact.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a synthetic task that aims to capture aspects of multi-hop reasoning, and uses it to characterize the behavior of Transformer models. It finds that pre-trained transformers learn the task more rapidly, and attributes this to specific patterns of attention in the network. Introducing mechanisms that emulate these specific attention patterns helps close this gap in performance without the need for expensive pre-training. Models struggle with extrapolating to longer chains of reasoning than required during training. This deterioration can be mitigated by repeating layers with the same parameters as well as training with stochastic depth.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The proposed task is useful as a tool for understanding some ways in which a Transformer can perform multi-hop reasoning
- The proposed operationalization of requiring length extrapolation is effective at disentangling issues with positional encoding from the question of whether the model can extrapolate to longer chains of reasoning 
- Experiments help provide an understanding for how pre-training, as well as stochastic depth, help improve certain aspects of the models' capabilities
- Hardcoded attention patterns are shown to be just as effective for solving this specific task
- The writing of the paper is clear and well-structured

Weaknesses:
- One of the strengths of the attention mechanism is that it can learn any attention pattern a task may require, and the multi-head version can learn when (at what layer) and how much to use each pattern. The hardcoded attention patterns seem sufficient for the proposed synthetic task -- almost by construction of the LEGO task. There are some preliminary findings that they may also be effective for masked language modeling and at least SQuAD, but this direction is underexplored and perhaps not all of the necessary components for real NLP tasks are captured.
- The proposed task is perhaps not the best test-bed of *pre-training* specifically, because the approach used is to pre-train on natural language but then test on synthetic expressions. It would not be surprising for language-specific reasoning to not undergo transfer in this case, potentially leaving only the most general of structural patterns, like the attention patterns revealed in this work.
- Overall, it's not clear how much of the Transformers' capabilities are stressed by the proposed synthetic task. It could be that understanding multi-hop computation in the style of the LEGO task is the key for explaining current behaviors and proposing improvements. Alternatively, multi-hop reasoning may just not be a crucial component of many NLP tasks as currently formulated, or LEGO might not be capturing the actual character of chain-based reasoning as required for real-world task.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
See above

Minor:
- Regarding Transformers that repeat layers: in addition to ALBERT, it would be helpful to cite other instances such as Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al)

Summary Of The Review:
My overall recommendation is based on weighing the thorough and well-written exposition/investigation of the LEGO task with the potential downsides of any synthetic benchmark, namely that it may or may not capture the key elements of actual real-world tasks, and can therefore be limited in its impact.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Revised Review**  

**Summary**  
This work introduces a synthetic benchmarking task (LEGO) designed to analyze multi-hop reasoning in Transformers. The study demonstrates that pre-trained models learn the task faster, linking this to specific learned attention patterns. Notably, manually encoding these attention patterns achieves comparable performance to pre-training. However, models fail to generalize to reasoning chains longer than those seen during training—a limitation partially addressed by parameter repetition and stochastic depth techniques.  

**Strengths**  
- The LEGO task provides a controlled framework for probing aspects of multi-hop reasoning in Transformers.  
- Length extrapolation is cleverly operationalized to isolate reasoning chain limitations from positional encoding issues.  
- Analyses of pre-training effects and stochastic depth offer empirical insights into architectural improvements.  
- Hardcoded attention mechanisms prove surprisingly effective for this task, suggesting potential alternatives to costly pre-training.  
- The paper is logically structured and clearly written.  

**Weaknesses**  
- While hardcoding attention patterns works well for LEGO, this approach circumvents a core strength of Transformers: their ability to dynamically learn context-specific attention strategies across heads and layers. The preliminary extension to SQuAD and language modeling is promising but underdeveloped, leaving open whether such patterns generalize to real-world tasks with diverse reasoning demands.  
- The pre-training evaluation setup (natural language pre-training → synthetic task fine-tuning) risks conflating generic structural biases (e.g., layered attention) with task-specific reasoning. This design choice may limit claims about how pre-training specifically contributes to multi-hop reasoning.  
- The broader relevance of LEGO remains unclear. While it isolates chain-of-reasoning mechanics, real-world NLP tasks may depend on orthogonal capabilities (e.g., semantic grounding, ambiguity resolution) not captured by the synthetic setup. This leaves uncertainty about whether improvements on LEGO translate to practical scenarios.  

**Additional Notes**  
- When discussing parameter-shared architectures (e.g., ALBERT), consider citing related works like Universal Transformers for completeness.  

**Recommendation**  
This paper presents a methodical investigation of multi-hop reasoning in Transformers, with valuable empirical insights into attention mechanisms and training strategies. However, the synthetic nature of LEGO—while a strength for controlled analysis—limits immediate practical implications, as its alignment with real-world reasoning demands remains unproven. The work narrowly exceeds the acceptance threshold due to its rigorous methodology and potential to inspire future architectural innovations.  

**Rating**: 6 (Marginally above acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper provides empirical investigation of transformer by constructing a synthetic reasoning task -- LEGO (Learning Equality and Group Operations). The LEGO tasks consist of group operations and a chain of reasoning. The major purpose of the synthetic experiment is to provide a controlled setting and unveil what transformer learns. To do so, the paper conduct a series of experiments on the both classic setting (training and testing distribution are the same) and the OOD setting (out of distribution) and they found

(1) Classical generalization happens all the architecture.

(2) OOD generalization depends on the architecture and data preparation.

(3) The non-extrapolating models (architecture fails for OOD) learns short-cut solution.

The author also propose hardcode attention layer that incorporates knowledge over the dataset that seems to work well on LEGO.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength. The paper provides solid contribution towards understanding the Transformer architecture. By constructing a synthetic benchmark, it provides a series experiment that provide new insight and confirm existing observations. 

Weakness. There is no major weakness.

Minor comment. The related work section can be improved, in particular, there is a previous line of work "unveils" transformer over regular language ( in particular dyck language) that should be cited and discussed. Examples include [1] (and see the reference therein), which studies the dyck language and provide some theory and empirical investigation on what transformer learns.

[1] Self-Attention Networks Can Process Bounded Hierarchical Languages. Shunyu Yao, Binghui Peng, Christos Papadimitriou, Karthik Narasimhan. ACL'2021
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Yes
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper provides solid contribution to the literature and I vote for acceptance.


Some minor questions over "shortcut":
The paper finds transformer sometime learns short-cut solution to the reasoning tasks. However, it is unclear to me why this is a bad thing. I feel it is not equivalent to spurious feature because some reasoning tasks involves short-cut solution or parallelizable solution, e.g., it is possible that a linear size circuit only has depth $O(\log n)$ (i.e., do not follow the chain of reasoning). Some further discussions should be a good addition on the finding!



------------

Post rebuttal

I have read the rebuttal and my evaluation remains.
